title: Executive Assistant

rationales.taskAutomation: Scheduling and correspondence are largely automatable, but confidential judgment calls, stakeholder diplomacy, and ambiguous prioritization for a specific executive still require a person.
rationales.toolMaturity: AI scheduling, email drafting, and briefing-prep tools are mature and widely embedded in executive productivity suites.
rationales.adoption: Adoption is common at large and tech-forward employers but slower where confidentiality and single-principal trust matter most.
rationales.agenticExposure: Agents can own discrete chains such as briefing assembly and travel logistics, but the trusted, principal-specific judgment core of the role resists autonomous ownership.

tasksAtRisk: Email screening, prioritization, and draft responses
Meeting briefing packets and materials preparation
tasksGrowing: Strategic executive support and decision prep
Directing and quality-checking AI-assisted work product
salarySource: BLS OES May 2024, SOC 43-6011 (Executive Secretaries and Executive Administrative Assistants), p25-p75; upper end reflects senior EA postings at large tech/finance employers
insight: AI now assembles the calendar, drafts the emails, and preps the briefing deck; what it can't do is read a principal's unstated priorities or manage the politics around them. EAs who move toward chief-of-staff-style trusted advising hold their ground better than those who stay purely logistical.
